en.extract:
The Boa Nova tapaculo (Scytalopus gonzagai), also known as the Bahian mouse-colored tapaculo,  is a species of passerine bird native to Bahia, Brazil.


Taxonomy
The Boa Nova tapaculo was first observed in the early 1990s, when it was misidentified as part of the Scytalopus speluncae species, or the mouse-coloured tapaculos.  A neighboring yet geographically isolated population was found in 1999.  In 2014, researchers from the Universidade Federal de Pelotas recognized Scytalopus gonzagai as a unique species.
The specific epithet was chosen to honour Luiz Antonio Pedreira Gonzaga, a Brazilian ornithologist who discovered two bird species in Bahia in the 1990s. Locals call it the "Macuquinho-preto-baiano".


Description
Covered in grey plumage and with yellow to brown claws, the species measures 12 centimetres (4.7 in) long and weighs 15 grams (0.53 oz).


Range and habitat
The species' range is limited to five patches of forest in mountainous parts of the Atlantic Forest in southern Bahia, Brazil, having been found in the municipalities of Arataca, Boa Nova, and Iguaí.  This is an area covering about 5,885 hectares.  S. gonzagai is usually found under dense vegetation or in branches no higher than 2 metres (6.6 ft) off the ground.


Behavior
Males are territorial, being found either on their own or as part of a pair with a female.  Females are typically only found paired with a male. The species has been observed to have three different kinds of call - a "kreew" sound, which probably serves as a sort of contact call, an alarm call, and the song.  Only females sing.  Distribution is usually about 0.49 individuals per hectare.


Conservation status
The Boa Nova tapaculo is estimated to consist of no more than 2,883 individuals, and is threatened by logging and deforestation, resulting in the scientists who described it urging that it be classified as an endangered species on the IUCN Red List.


References

 Data related to Scytalopus gonzagai at Wikispecies

The '''Boa Nova tapaculo''' ('''''Scytalopus gonzagai'''''), also known as the '''Bahian mouse-colored tapaculo''',<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Maurício | first1=G.N. | last2=Belmonte-Lopes | first2=R. | last3=Pacheco | first3=J.F. | last4=Silveira | first4=L.F. | last5=Whitney | first5=B.M. | last6=Bornschein | first6=M.R. | date=2015 | title=Erratum: Taxonomy of "Mouse-colored Tapaculos" (II): An endangered new species from the montane Atlantic Forest of southern Bahia, Brazil (Passeriformes: Rhinocryptidae: ''Scytalopus'') | journal=The Auk | volume=132 | issue=4 | pages=951–952 | doi=10.1642/AUK-15-123.1 | doi-access=free }}</ref>  is a [[species]] of [[passerine]] [[bird]] native to [[Bahia]], [[Brazil]].

==Taxonomy==
The Boa Nova tapaculo was first observed in the early 1990s, when it was misidentified as part of the ''[[Scytalopus speluncae]]'' species, or the mouse-coloured tapaculos.  A neighboring yet geographically isolated population was found in 1999.  In 2014, researchers from the [[Universidade Federal de Pelotas]] recognized ''Scytalopus gonzagai'' as a unique species.<ref name=mauricio2014>{{Cite journal | last1=Maurício | first1=G.N. | last2=Belmonte-Lopes | first2=R. | last3=Pacheco | first3=J.F. | last4=Silveira | first4=L.F. | last5=Whitney | first5=B.M. | last6=Bornschein | first6=M.R. | date=2014 | title=Taxonomy of "Mouse-colored Tapaculos" (II): An endangered new species from the montane Atlantic Forest of southern Bahia, Brazil (Passeriformes: Rhinocryptidae: ''Scytalopus'') | journal=The Auk | volume=131 | issue=4 | pages=643–659 | doi=10.1642/AUK-14-16.1 | doi-access=free }}</ref>

The specific epithet was chosen to honour Luiz Antonio Pedreira Gonzaga, a Brazilian [[ornithologist]] who discovered two bird species in Bahia in the 1990s. Locals call it the "Macuquinho-preto-baiano".<ref name=mauricio2014/>

==Description==
Covered in grey plumage and with yellow to brown claws, the species measures {{convert|12|cm|in}} long and weighs {{convert|15|g|oz}}.<ref name=mauricio2014/>

==Range and habitat==
The species' range is limited to five patches of forest<ref name="SciAm">{{cite web |url=http://blogs.scientificamerican.com/extinction-countdown/2014/09/29/deforestation-new-bird-brazil/ |title=Deforestation Threatens Newly Identified Bird in Brazil |first=Platt |last=John R. |date=29 September 2014 |website=[[Scientific American]] |access-date=30 September 2014 |archive-date=6 October 2014 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141006070241/http://blogs.scientificamerican.com/extinction-countdown/2014/09/29/deforestation-new-bird-brazil/ |url-status=live }}</ref> in mountainous parts of the [[Atlantic Forest]] in southern [[Bahia]], [[Brazil]], having been found in the [[Municipalities of Brazil|municipalities]] of [[Arataca]], [[Boa Nova]], and [[Iguaí]].  This is an area covering about 5,885 [[hectare]]s.  ''S. gonzagai'' is usually found under dense vegetation or in branches no higher than {{convert|2|m|ft}} off the ground.<ref name="scinews">{{cite journal |url=http://www.sci-news.com/biology/science-bahian-mouse-colored-tapaculo-scytalopus-gonzagai-new-bird-species-brazil-02164.html |title=Bahian Mouse-Colored Tapaculo: New Bird Species Discovered in Brazil |author=Natali Anderson |date=22 September 2014 |website=Sci-News.com |access-date=23 September 2014 |doi=10.1642/AUK-14-16.1 |s2cid=85700287 |doi-access=free |archive-date=23 September 2014 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140923120114/http://www.sci-news.com/biology/science-bahian-mouse-colored-tapaculo-scytalopus-gonzagai-new-bird-species-brazil-02164.html |url-status=live }}</ref>

==Behavior==
Males are territorial, being found either on their own or as part of a pair with a female.  Females are typically only found paired with a male. The species has been observed to have three different kinds of [[Bird vocalization|call]] - a "kreew" sound, which probably serves as a sort of contact call, an alarm call, and the song.  Only females sing.  [[Species distribution|Distribution]] is usually about 0.49 individuals per hectare.<ref name="scinews" />

==Conservation status==
The Boa Nova tapaculo is estimated to consist of no more than 2,883 individuals,<ref name="scinews" /> and is threatened by [[logging]] and [[Deforestation in Brazil|deforestation]],<ref name="SciAm" /> resulting in the scientists who described it urging that it be classified as an [[endangered species]] on the [[IUCN Red List]].<ref name="scinews" />
